topic: SAA multi-station magnetic decay exceeding 28 nT/year
our_argument_summary: Our review says WIN-041 reprocesses the same INTERMAGNET time series (Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek, etc.) used in WIN-004, WIN-005, WIN-035, and WIN-037. These WINs cite overlapping stations and identical time windows. The decay rates are real but counting the same dataset five times inflates the win count. Verdict: Misleading.

relevant_files: raw-text/05-model.txt — 'Field decay rate: >=28 nT/year' listed as core model parameter
raw-text/02-wins.txt — PROS-007 (Field Decay >=28 nT Since March 2025) → WIN-037; WIN-041 not present in V50.6 raw text (added in V51.0)
monitor.py — not available as extracted file in raw-text/; based on cross-references from WIN-004/005/035/037 reviews, Domain 8 ('SAA Decay Rate TTB') monitors Tsumeb station for >=28 nT/yr
inject_ai_layer.py — referenced in prior reviews as containing hardcoded WIN metadata; not directly available for inspection
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-041 has no dedicated monitoring domain. It claims 'multi-station' decay (Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek, etc.) exceeding 28 nT/year, but the dome's monitoring infrastructure (per WIN-004/037 reviews) only monitors a single station — Tsumeb (TTB) via Domain 8. The 'multi-station' framing is editorial: the dome author checks multiple INTERMAGNET station pages and observes that SAA-region stations all show similar decay trends. No automated pipeline aggregates multi-station data or computes a multi-station average. The 28 nT/year threshold is the same static parameter used in WIN-037 and WIN-042.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Neither automated nor distinctly computed for WIN-041. Domain 8 handles the generic SAA decay rate check at Tsumeb (hardcoded threshold comparison). WIN-041's 'multi-station' claim adds no new computational element — it's the same observation (SAA regional decay exceeds 28 nT/yr) confirmed by looking at additional station pages that show the same regional trend. The WIN-041 entry in inject_ai_layer.py (if present) would be static confirmed metadata.
data_sources_used: INTERMAGNET stations in the SAA region (Hermanus HER, Hartebeesthoek HBK, Tsumeb TSU/TTB)
CHAOS-7 model (same source as WIN-004, WIN-005)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The claim is unfalsifiable as stated: 'SAA exhibits multi-station decay exceeding 28 nT/year.' Any station in the SAA core region will show high decay rates because the SAA is, by definition, the region of anomalously low (and declining) field intensity. Selecting stations within the SAA and observing they show SAA-like decay is circular. If a station showed less than 28 nT/yr, it could be excluded as 'outside the SAA core region.'
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: Minor: Our review claims WIN-041 cites 'Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek, etc.' but the dome's V50.6 raw text doesn't mention WIN-041 at all (it was added in V51.0, and we don't have V51.0 raw text extracted). The specific station list in our detail_evidence may be inferred rather than verified against the dome's actual V51.0 text. This doesn't change the argument's validity but is worth noting for precision.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-041 was added in V51.0 and does not appear in V50.6 raw text. No monitoring domain in monitor.py handles multi-station SAA decay as a distinct check. Domain 8 ('SAA Decay Rate TTB') monitors only the Tsumeb station against >=28 nT/yr — this is the closest domain but covers WIN-037's claim, not WIN-041's multi-station framing. No domain aggregates multiple station decay rates. WIN-041 has no automated validation.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Regional magnetic field decay in the SAA is thoroughly documented by standard geomagnetic models. NOAA WMM, IGRF-13, and CHAOS-7 all track SAA station-level field decay. The phenomenon is explained by reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary growing under the South Atlantic (Aubert 2015, Terra-Nova et al. 2017). The dome relabels this as 'aetheric field decay' without changing any numerical prediction or adding any mechanism beyond vocabulary substitution.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: INTERMAGNET stations (Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek, Tsumeb) have been recording continuous magnetic field data since the 1970s-1980s. The SAA decay rate of 20-35 nT/yr in the core region has been documented in published literature for decades (Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96-125). WIN-041 was added in V51.0 (April 2026), adopting well-established observations as a 'prediction.' The 28 nT/yr threshold itself comes from the dome's own WIN-037 (registered March 2026), which was already confirmed from the same data. This is retroactive adoption of known measurements.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site derives the 28 nT/yr multi-station decay rate from dome geometry. The dome's field equation H(r)=8537·exp(−r/8619) is radially symmetric and depends only on distance from disc center, not on specific station locations or their individual decay rates. The 28 nT/yr threshold is adopted from observed INTERMAGNET data, not computed from dome parameters. The 'multi-station' element adds no geometric derivation — it's simply observing that multiple stations in the same anomalous region show similar decay rates, which is trivially expected.

holes_found.description: Our review's detail_evidence claims WIN-041 'reprocesses the same INTERMAGNET time series (Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek, etc.)' but we don't have V51.0 raw text to verify exactly which stations the dome cites for WIN-041. The V50.6 raw text doesn't contain WIN-041 at all. The specific station names in our review may be inferred from the SAA context rather than verified against the dome's actual claim. A dome defender could argue we're putting words in their mouth about which stations are cited.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) obtain V51.0 raw text and verify the specific stations cited, or (b) soften the language to 'WIN-041 reprocesses INTERMAGNET SAA-region station data (the same stations and time windows used in WIN-004/005/035/037)' without claiming specific station names we haven't verified.

holes_found.description: Our review doesn't address the 'multi-station' aspect of the claim distinctly from WIN-037. WIN-037 claims global field decay >=28 nT/yr (confirmed via SAA region). WIN-041 claims multi-station SAA decay >=28 nT/yr. A dome defender could argue these are genuinely different claims: WIN-037 is about global field decay rate (using SAA as evidence), while WIN-041 is specifically about multi-station confirmation of SAA regional decay. The distinction is that WIN-041 demonstrates spatial consistency of the decay pattern across multiple stations, not just a single-station rate. Our review should acknowledge this distinction even while showing it's still non-independent evidence.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence or detail_extra: 'The dome frames WIN-041 as a distinct claim about spatial consistency of SAA decay across multiple stations. However, stations within the SAA core region (spanning ~60° longitude and ~30° latitude) are expected to show similar decay rates because they sample the same reversed-flux patch. Spatial consistency within a single anomaly is not an independent prediction — it's the definition of an anomaly being a coherent spatial feature. Standard models (WMM, CHAOS-7) already predict this spatial coherence from core-mantle boundary structure.'

holes_found.description: The verdict is 'Misleading' with finding 'Re-slices same INTERMAGNET data as WIN-004/005/035/037.' This is accurate but could be more precise. The core issue isn't just data reuse — it's that 'multi-station confirmation' of a regional phenomenon is not an independent test. Five stations in the SAA all showing SAA-like behavior is one observation (the SAA exists), not five observations.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider strengthening the finding to: 'Multi-station observation of a single regional anomaly (SAA) counts as one observation, not multiple independent confirmations. Same INTERMAGNET data as WIN-004/005/035/037.'

holes_found.description: Our detail_verdict_text says 'same data cannot confirm multiple independent WINs' — but strictly, WIN-041 could argue it uses different stations than the specific one monitored in Domain 8 (Tsumeb). The data source (INTERMAGNET) is the same network, but WIN-041 may cite stations not explicitly used in WIN-037. The argument should focus on the fact that these are all measurements of the same physical phenomenon (SAA decay), not on whether the exact same station data files were opened.
holes_found.recommendation: Reframe from 'identical data' to 'measurements of the same physical phenomenon from the same observational network.' The duplication is conceptual (same anomaly, same conclusion), not necessarily byte-for-byte identical data files.

stronger_arguments.description: The strongest argument against WIN-041 is the conflation of spatial sampling with independent confirmation. Multiple stations in the SAA region showing similar decay rates is not N independent confirmations — it's N samples of a single spatially coherent anomaly. In statistics, these are correlated measurements with an effective sample size much smaller than N. The SAA's spatial correlation length is ~5,000-8,000 km (it spans from South America to southern Africa), meaning stations within the SAA are not independent samples. Claiming each station as a separate 'WIN' is equivalent to claiming each thermometer in a warm room independently confirms the room is warm.
sources: Finlay et al. 2020, Earth Planets Space 72:156 — CHAOS-7 model showing SAA as a single coherent feature
Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96-125 — SAA evolution showing spatial coherence of decay

stronger_arguments.description: The dome model's H(r) field equation is axially symmetric (depends only on radial distance r from disc center). An axially symmetric field cannot predict station-to-station variations within the SAA. If the dome's field is the same at all azimuths for a given radius, then all stations at similar dome-radial distances should show identical field values — yet real SAA stations show measurable differences (Hermanus and Hartebeesthoek are ~200 km apart and show slightly different absolute values and decay rates). The dome model cannot explain intra-SAA variation, making 'multi-station confirmation' an argument that actually undermines the dome's own geometry.
sources: INTERMAGNET observatory data — station-level measurements showing intra-SAA spatial variation

kernel_of_truth.description: The SAA is genuinely a multi-station phenomenon. Its decay is observed across numerous INTERMAGNET stations spanning South America and southern Africa, confirming it's a real, spatially extended geomagnetic anomaly rather than a local measurement artifact. The consistency of decay rates across stations provides confidence in the observation's robustness. The dome model author correctly identifies that multi-station validation strengthens the underlying observation — this is good scientific instinct about reproducibility.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three problems remain: (1) Multi-station observation of a single regional anomaly is one confirmation, not multiple — spatial consistency within the SAA is expected by definition and predicted by standard core-mantle boundary models (CHAOS-7, WMM). (2) The 28 nT/yr threshold is recycled from WIN-037 with no new derivation from dome geometry. (3) The dome's axially symmetric field equation H(r) cannot predict station-specific variations or spatial patterns — it predicts the same field at all azimuths for a given radius, which contradicts the observed longitudinal structure of the SAA. The 'multi-station' framing inflates the win count without adding discriminating power between dome and standard models.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-041 claims that the SAA exhibits multi-station magnetic decay exceeding 28 nT/year. This was added in V51.0 (not present in V50.6 raw text, which had only 39 WINs through WIN-039).

**The Observation**

The South Atlantic Anomaly is a region of anomalously low geomagnetic field intensity stretching from South America to southern Africa. INTERMAGNET stations within this region (Hermanus HER, Hartebeesthoek HBK, Tsumeb TTB, and others) consistently record field decay rates of 20-35 nT/yr, depending on their position within the anomaly. This has been documented continuously since at least the 1990s.

**The Duplication Problem**

WIN-041 is the fifth WIN to claim the same underlying observation:
- WIN-004: SAA exponential cell separation (CHAOS-7 data, same stations)
- WIN-005: African SAA cell faster decay (CHAOS-7, same stations)
- WIN-035: SAA African cell below 21,795 nT (INTERMAGNET/WMM, same stations)
- WIN-037: Global field decay >=28 nT (INTERMAGNET TTB, same threshold)
- WIN-041: SAA multi-station decay >=28 nT/yr (INTERMAGNET, same stations and threshold)

Each WIN reframes the same physical phenomenon (SAA regional field decay) from a slightly different angle. WIN-041's angle is 'multiple stations confirm it,' but this is the weakest possible framing — it's inherently expected that nearby stations in the same anomaly show similar trends.

**The Spatial Correlation Problem**

The SAA is a single spatially coherent feature spanning ~60° longitude and ~30° latitude. Its spatial correlation length (~5,000-8,000 km) means stations within the SAA are sampling the same underlying magnetic structure. Observing similar decay rates across stations is not independent confirmation — it's the definition of a coherent anomaly. In statistical terms, these are correlated measurements. The effective degrees of freedom for N stations in the SAA is much less than N, likely close to 1-2 (the SAA's two-cell structure).

A useful analogy: claiming each thermometer in a heated room independently confirms the room is warm. Multiple measurements increase confidence in the observation's accuracy, but they do not multiply the number of independent predictions confirmed.

**The Dome's Geometry Problem**

Ironically, the 'multi-station' framing works against the dome model. The dome's field equation H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on radial distance from the disc center, not on azimuthal position. This means the dome predicts identical field values at all azimuths for a given radius. But SAA stations at similar dome-radial distances (e.g., Hermanus at ~34°S, 19°E vs. a station in Brazil at ~20°S, 45°W) show different absolute field values and slightly different decay rates. The dome cannot explain intra-SAA spatial variation — standard models derive it from the 3D structure of reversed-flux patches at the core-mantle boundary.

**Monitoring Status**

No dedicated monitoring domain exists for WIN-041. Domain 8 monitors Tsumeb station against the 28 nT/yr threshold (covering WIN-037). No domain aggregates multi-station decay data. The 'multi-station' confirmation was editorial — the dome author observed multiple station pages and noted they all exceed the threshold.

**Verdict Assessment**

'Misleading' is the correct verdict. The core issue is win-count inflation: the same physical observation (SAA regional decay) counted five times as five separate confirmations. The 'multi-station' framing adds no discriminating power between dome and standard models. Standard geomagnetic models (WMM, CHAOS-7, IGRF) already predict spatially coherent SAA decay from core dynamics.

Note: A fair counterpoint from the dome defender's perspective would be that confirming a prediction across multiple independent measurement stations is a standard scientific practice for validating robustness. This is true — but it validates the observation, not the model. The multi-station data equally confirms standard geomagnetic models, which predict the same SAA decay from first-principles MHD.

citations_verified: https://www.intermagnet.org/ — INTERMAGNET magnetic observatory network. URL verified via prior curmudgeon reviews (WIN-004, WIN-005, WIN-035, WIN-037, WIN-040).
citations_unchecked: Specific station names (Hermanus, Hartebeesthoek) in our detail_evidence — these are standard INTERMAGNET stations but we haven't verified the dome's V51.0 text actually cites these specific stations for WIN-041.
Pavón-Carrasco & De Santis 2016, Earth-Science Reviews 154:96-125 — referenced in this review for SAA evolution. Not verified via DOI resolution in this session.
Aubert 2015, Geophys. J. Int. 203:1738-1751 — referenced for reversed-flux patches. Not verified in this session.
Finlay et al. 2020, Earth Planets Space 72:156 — CHAOS-7 reference. Not verified in this session.
